note: Australian-produced. Grounded in the course's own modules. Founder to review before wiring live.

quizzes.title: Dust and Particulates in Indoor Air: Control and Cleaning Strategies — Quiz

questions.questionText: According to the course, how is particulate matter (PM) classified?
options: By their aerodynamic diameter
By their colour under ultraviolet light
By their chemical odour
By the electrical charge they carry

questions.questionText: How does the course define ultrafine particles (UFPs)?
options: Coarse particles 10 micrometres or less, such as pollen and mould spores
Fine particles 2.5 micrometres or smaller, mainly from combustion
Particles smaller than 0.1 micrometres that can penetrate the deepest regions of the lungs and enter the bloodstream
Visible household dust that settles quickly on surfaces

questions.questionText: Why can PM2.5 and ultrafine particles cause deeper harm than larger particles?
options: They are caught in the nasal passages and expelled by sneezing
They penetrate deep into the lungs and reach the alveoli, where they can cause inflammation and be absorbed into the bloodstream
They are too large to be inhaled into the airways
They dissolve harmlessly in saliva before reaching the lungs
questions.sourceRef: M2 — Health Impacts of Dust and Fine Particles

questions.questionText: Why does the course identify children as particularly vulnerable to particulates?
options: Their respiratory systems are still developing and they breathe more air per kilogram of body weight than adults
They are exposed only to coarse PM10 particles
Their immune systems are fully mature and overreact to dust
They spend far less time indoors than adults
questions.sourceRef: M2 — Health Impacts of Dust and Fine Particles

questions.questionText: What does the course describe as 'resuspension'?
options: Dust particles clumping together and settling in high humidity
The permanent settling of heavy particles into carpets
Previously settled dust being lifted back into the air by activities such as walking, sweeping, or vacuuming
The removal of dust as air passes through an HVAC filter
questions.sourceRef: M3 — How Dust Moves Through Indoor Environments

questions.questionText: How does low humidity affect airborne dust, according to the course?
options: It allows particles to remain airborne for longer
It causes particles to clump together and settle more quickly
It increases microbial growth on suspended particles
It has no measurable effect on how dust behaves
questions.sourceRef: M3 — How Dust Moves Through Indoor Environments

questions.questionText: The course states that a PM2.5 reading above which level is considered unhealthy for sensitive groups?
options: 12 µg/m³
35 µg/m³
100 µg/m³
2.5 µg/m³
questions.sourceRef: M4 — Measuring Dust Levels and Airborne Particles

questions.questionText: What does the MERV rating describe?
options: A filter's ability to trap particles of different sizes
The volume of airflow a fan can produce
The expected lifespan of a filter in months
The overall energy efficiency of an HVAC unit
